summary: Lex Fridman interviews Joel David Hamkins on topics in the foundations of mathematics, including infinity, paradoxes, Gödel's incompleteness theorems, and the set-theoretic multiverse. Hamkins also discusses his work on MathOverflow and infinite chess.

main_entities.name: Infinity
main_entities.context: Discussed different sizes of infinity, countable and uncountable infinities, and the historical development of the concept.

main_entities.name: Cantor's Theorem
main_entities.context: The theorem that the power set of any set is strictly larger than the set itself.

main_entities.name: ZFC (Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory with the axiom of choice)
main_entities.context: Discussed as the standard foundation for most modern mathematics, consisting of a set of axioms.

main_entities.name: Russell's Paradox
main_entities.context: The paradox arising from the set of all sets that do not contain themselves.

main_entities.name: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems
main_entities.context: The theorems stating that no consistent formal system can prove all truths about arithmetic and cannot prove its own consistency.

main_entities.name: Hilbert's Program
main_entities.context: Hilbert's early 20th-century project to give all of classical mathematics a completely secure finitary foundation.

main_entities.name: Halting Problem
main_entities.context: The problem of determining whether a given program will halt or run forever.

main_entities.name: Surreal Numbers
main_entities.context: A number system introduced by John Conway that unifies all other number systems.

main_entities.name: Axiom of Choice
main_entities.context: A fundamental principle in set theory which states that for any collection of non-empty sets, it is possible to select exactly one element from each set.

main_entities.name: MathOverflow
main_entities.context: A question and answer website for research mathematicians.

main_entities.name: Infinite Chess
main_entities.context: Chess played on an infinite board.

main_entities.name: Forcing
main_entities.context: A technique for building alternative mathematical realities.

main_entities.name: Set-Theoretic Multiverse
main_entities.context: The idea that there may not be one true mathematics, but rather multiple mathematical universes.

key_details.fact: Cantor proved that some infinities are larger than others, leading to a theological crisis and mathematical civil war.
Hilbert's Hotel is a thought experiment illustrating the properties of countable infinity.
The real numbers are uncountable, meaning they cannot be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers.
ZFC consists of axioms like extensionality, empty set, pairing, union, power set, infinity, separation, replacement, regularity, and choice.
Gödel's incompleteness theorems refuted Hilbert's program by showing that no computably axiomatizable theory can answer all questions or prove its own consistency.
Tarski's disquotational theory of truth states that the sentence "Snow is white" is true if and only if snow is white.
The halting problem is computably undecidable, meaning there is no computable procedure to determine whether a given program will halt.
The continuum hypothesis is independent of ZFC, meaning it cannot be proved or disproved within the ZFC axioms.
The surreal numbers are generated from nothing by a single rule and unify all other number systems.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Cantor
other_persons_mentioned.context: Developed the idea that some infinities are bigger than others and struggled with the continuum hypothesis.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Kronecker
other_persons_mentioned.context: Called Cantor a corrupter of youth and tried to block his career.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Aristotle
other_persons_mentioned.context: Emphasized the potential aspect of infinity.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Archimedes
other_persons_mentioned.context: Used the method of exhaustion to understand area.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Galileo
other_persons_mentioned.context: Anticipated Cantor's developments but couldn't push it all the way through.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Euclid
other_persons_mentioned.context: Appealed to the principle that the whole is always greater than the part.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Hilbert
other_persons_mentioned.context: Proposed a program to fix the problems in set theory.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Zermelo
other_persons_mentioned.context: Introduced most of the axioms of Zermelo set theory.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Fraenkel
other_persons_mentioned.context: Co-namesake of Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Russell
other_persons_mentioned.context: Discovered Russell's paradox.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Frege
other_persons_mentioned.context: Attempted to reduce all of mathematics to logic.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Gödel
other_persons_mentioned.context: Proved the incompleteness theorems.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Tarski
other_persons_mentioned.context: Gave an account of the nature of truth.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Turing
other_persons_mentioned.context: Developed computability theory and proved the undecidability of the halting problem.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Cohen
other_persons_mentioned.context: Invented the method of forcing and proved the independence of the continuum hypothesis.

other_persons_mentioned.name: John Conway
other_persons_mentioned.context: Introduced the surreal number system.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Hugh Woodin
other_persons_mentioned.context: Prominent mathematician with the universe view.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Benedikt Loewe
other_persons_mentioned.context: Collaborated with Hamkins on the modal logic of forcing.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Gunter Fuchs
other_persons_mentioned.context: Collaborated with Hamkins on set-theoretic geology.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Jonas Riets
other_persons_mentioned.context: Hamkins' PhD student who worked on set-theoretic geology.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Alexey Myasnikov
other_persons_mentioned.context: Asked Hamkins about decision problems with a black hole.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Pólya
other_persons_mentioned.context: Developed the Pólya recurrence theorem.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Andrew Wiles
other_persons_mentioned.context: Proved Fermat's Last Theorem.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Terence Tao
other_persons_mentioned.context: Mathematician who switches problems when hitting a wall.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Grigori Perelman
other_persons_mentioned.context: Turned down the Fields Medal and the Millennial Prize.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Donald Knuth
other_persons_mentioned.context: Tried to celebrate the surreal numbers.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Philip Ehrlich
other_persons_mentioned.context: Expert on the surreal numbers.
